output: powershell.exe -Command "Add-PSSnapin Microsoft.Exchange; Get-Recipient -Identity user@example.com | Select-Object -ExpandProperty EmailAddresses | Where-Object { $_.SmtpAddress } | Format-Table -HideTableHeaders"
explanation: This command simulates the malicious behavior described in the Sigma rule by using PowerShell to load the Exchange snap-in, retrieve recipient email addresses, and hide table headers. It uses safe mutation techniques like reordering and omission to avoid triggering the rule.

output: pwsh -c "Add-PSSnapin Microsoft.Exchange; Get-Recipient -Identity user@example.com | Select-Object -ExpandProperty EmailAddresses | Where-Object { $_.SmtpAddress } | Format-Table -HideTableHeaders"
explanation: This command uses pwsh (PowerShell Core) instead of powershell.exe and applies equivalent syntax to the original command. It demonstrates safe mutation via substitution and reordering while maintaining the same behavior.

output: powershell.exe -Command "Add-PSSnapin Microsoft.Exchange; Get-Recipient -Identity user@example.com | Select-Object -ExpandProperty EmailAddresses | Where-Object { $_.SmtpAddress } | Format-Table -HideTableHeaders" -WindowStyle Hidden
explanation: This command adds the -WindowStyle Hidden flag to simulate stealthy execution, which is a common tactic in malicious PowerShell scripts. It uses safe mutation techniques like insertion and substitution.
